sections.title: 1. பாடச் சுருக்கம் (Full Summary of the Poem)

content.text: The poem 'Indian Seasons' by Nisha Dygrine beautifully describes the changing seasons in India.
Summer: Summer begins with intense, bright heat ('blaze of heat'). It brings cheerfulness represented by 'sunny smiles', but the dry weather results in 'dusty feet' due to the lack of rain.
Monsoon: The seasons then shift to the rainy season (Monsoons). This season is characterized by muddy roads. It brings delicious mangoes and is a time when leapfrogs and toads are seen everywhere.
Spring: Spring is described as 'pretty' but it is very 'short and sweet'. During this time, one can enjoy the fresh, sweet smell of green grass while sitting on a garden seat.

Autumn: The poet contrasts English Autumn with Indian Autumn. In England, autumn is colorful with leaves turning red, yellow, and brown. In India, autumn is simply identified as the time when leaves dry and fall down from the trees.

sections.title: 2. கவிதையின் வரிகள் மற்றும் கூடுதல் குறிப்புகள் (Poem Lines & Additional Notes)

content.text: Stanza 1: Summer comes / in a blaze of heat with / sunny smiles / and dusty feet.
Stanza 2: Then seasons change / to muddy roads / monsoons and mangoes / leapfrogs and toads.
Stanza 3: Spring is pretty / but short and sweet / when you can smell the grass / from your garden seat.
Stanza 4: Autumn is English / in red, yellow and brown / Autumn is Indian / Whenever leaves fell down.
Author: The poem is written by Nisha Dygrine.
The poem highlights how Indian seasons differ slightly from western seasons, particularly Autumn.

sections.title: 3. நயங்கள் மற்றும் இலக்கணக் குறிப்புகள் (Figures of Speech & Poetic Devices)

content.text: Rhyming Words: heat - feet (Stanza 1), roads - toads (Stanza 2), sweet - seat (Stanza 3), brown - down (Stanza 4).
Rhyme Scheme: The rhyme scheme for each stanza is a b c b.
Alliteration (மோனை): Words starting with the same consonant sound in a line. Examples: 'Sunny Smiles' (s, s), 'Monsoons and Mangoes' (m, m), 'Short and Sweet' (s, s).
Imagery (உருவகம்): The poet uses strong visual and sensory imagery like 'blaze of heat', 'smell the grass', and 'red, yellow and brown'.
Blaze - Bright flame or fire / intense heat (கடும் வெப்பம்)
Sunny - Bright with sunlight (சூரிய ஒளி நிரம்பிய)
Dusty - Covered with dust (புழுதி படிந்த)
Monsoons - The rainy season (பருவமழை)
Leapfrogs - Frogs that jump (குதிக்கும் தவளைகள்)
Toads - Tailless amphibians similar to frogs but with drier, warty skin (தேரைகள்)
Pretty - Beautiful (அழகான)
Autumn - The season after summer and before winter, when leaves fall (இலையுதிர் காலம்)

sections.title: 5. நினைவில் கொள்க (Points to Remember for Exams)

content.text: There are four seasons mentioned in the poem: Summer, Monsoon, Spring, and Autumn.
Summer is associated with 'sunny smiles' and 'dusty feet'.
Monsoon is associated with 'muddy roads', 'mangoes', 'leapfrogs', and 'toads'.
Spring is described as 'short and sweet'.
English Autumn features leaves in colors of red, yellow, and brown.
Indian Autumn is simply recognized when the leaves fall down.

sections.title: C. Read the lines and answer the questions given below.

content.text: 1. Summer comes in a blaze of heat with sunny smiles and dusty feet.
a) Does the poet welcome the summer? How do you know? - Answer: Yes, the poet welcomes the summer because she describes it with 'sunny smiles', which gives a positive and cheerful feeling.
b) Which line tells you that there is no rain in summer? - Answer: The line 'and dusty feet' tells us that the ground is dry and there is no rain.
2. Spring is pretty but short and sweet when you can smell the grass from your garden seat.
a) How does the poet describe the spring season? - Answer: The poet describes the spring season as pretty, short, and sweet.
b) Which line tells you that the garden is fresh? - Answer: The line 'when you can smell the grass' tells us that the garden is fresh.
c) Who does 'you' refer to? - Answer: 'You' refers to the reader.
3. Autumn is English in red, yellow and brown Autumn is Indian Whenever leaves fell down.
a) How is autumn in India? - Answer: In India, autumn is the season when leaves dry and fall down from the trees.

b) Compare the English autumn with the Indian autumn. - Answer: In English autumn, the leaves change into vibrant colors like red, yellow, and brown before falling. In Indian autumn, leaves simply wither and fall down.

sections.title: D. Pick out the rhyming words from the poem.

content.text: Stanza 1: heat - feet
Stanza 2: roads - toads
Stanza 3: sweet - seat
Stanza 4: brown - down

sections.title: E. Find examples of alliteration and write them.

content.text: 1. in a blaze of heat with sunny smiles -> Answer: sunny, smiles (s, s)
2. to muddy roads monsoons and mangoes -> Answer: monsoons, mangoes (m, m)
3. Spring is pretty but short and sweet -> Answer: short, sweet (s, s)

sections.title: F. Work in pairs, find answers for the questions.

content.text: 1. Name the seasons mentioned in the poem? - Answer: Summer, Monsoon (Rainy), Spring, and Autumn.
2. What does the summer bring? - Answer: Summer brings a blaze of heat, sunny smiles, and dusty feet.
3. Which word refers to 'rain'? - Answer: The word 'monsoons' refers to rain.
4. Why does the poet say the 'Spring is pretty'? - Answer: Because during spring, one can sit in the garden and smell the fresh, sweet grass.

5. When is it autumn in India? Why does the poet say this? Is there an autumn season in India? - Answer: It is autumn in India when the leaves fall down. The poet says this because Indian trees simply shed their leaves without changing into vibrant colors like western countries. Yes, there is a brief autumn season in India before winter.
